name: Thorne
short_description: BLM public land in Nevada's Mineral County offering free rockhounding for quartz, agate, and chalcedony specimens. High elevation site with moderate terrain and no permit requirements.
description:
Thorne is a public BLM rockhounding site located in Mineral County, Nevada at 5,396 feet elevation in the high desert region. This area is part of Nevada's Basin and Range province, where Tertiary volcanic and hydrothermal activity created abundant silica-rich deposits. The moderate slopes and sagebrush terrain make for pleasant rockhounding conditions, with opportunities to find quality quartz specimens, agate nodules, and jasper throughout the dispersed collecting area.

The geology here reflects Nevada's complex volcanic history. Miocene-age rhyolites and related igneous rocks host secondary silica mineralizations that weathered and concentrated into the agates and chalcedony you'll find at surface level. The higher elevation keeps the area cooler than lower Nevada deserts, making fall and spring visits particularly comfortable.

Rockhounders visiting Thorne should expect to search wash bottoms, exposed hillsides, and weathered talus slopes where specimens weather out naturally. The BLM designation means free, unrestricted access for personal use collection with no permit required. Most collectors find small to medium-sized specimens suitable for lapidary work or display collections.

Access is straightforward from Mineral County, and the moderate terrain is suitable for families with older children and casual rockhounders. Be prepared for high desert conditions including sun exposure, variable weather, and limited services nearby. The remote location offers solitude and productive collecting for those willing to explore systematically.
history: This area sits within the Basin and Range province where Tertiary volcanic activity (primarily Miocene age, roughly 20-25 million years ago) deposited silica-rich fluids and formed the quartz, agate, and jasper deposits found here today. The region saw active mining and prospecting during Nevada's mineral booms, and the high desert terrain preserves abundant secondary silica minerals exposed through weathering and erosion.
tips: Search wash bottoms and erosional draws where agate nodules and quartz specimens naturally concentrate and weather out from surrounding matrix. Look for distinctive banded agate patterns and translucent chalcedony - specimens often appear on the surface after seasonal rain events. Use a classifier or fine-mesh screen to sift wash sediments for smaller specimens missed by visual inspection. Bring plenty of water and sun protection as the high elevation and exposed terrain offer minimal shade.
terrain_notes: Elevation 5,396 feet with moderate slopes covered in sagebrush and desert scrub. Terrain is rocky with loose volcanic material and scattered outcrops. Walking distance from parking varies by chosen collecting area, typically 0.5 to 2 miles. Footing is uneven and rocky but not steep. No developed trails; cross-country travel required. Late spring snowmelt can create temporary washout conditions.
best_season: April - May and September - October. The high elevation (5,396 ft) makes summers hot and winters cold, but spring and fall offer comfortable temperatures. Winter snow can close access. Summer sun is intense at this elevation.
written_directions: From Hawthorne, Nevada (the nearest town with services), take Nevada State Route 95 north approximately 25 miles toward Mina. Watch for BLM access points and rough dirt roads branching east into the Mineral County rangelands. GPS coordinates 38.61894, -118.506446 will guide you to the Thorne area. High-clearance vehicle recommended for access roads; some washing or rough patches may require 4WD depending on recent weather. Parking available at pullouts along access roads; arrive early during popular seasons.
road_conditions: Rough dirt access roads with potential washboard surface. High-clearance vehicle recommended. After wet weather, some sections may require 4WD. No maintained highway to site; BLM roads may be unimproved.
parking_notes: Dispersed parking at informal pullouts along BLM access roads. No developed parking lot. Space for 5-10 vehicles at typical spots. No facilities. Arrive early to secure good parking during peak collecting seasons (spring and fall).
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection permitted. No motorized excavation. No removal of specimens from active mining claims or posted private property. Collecting limited to loose surface material; do not excavate or break into solid rock formations. Pack out all trash. Leave site as you found it.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
No removal from active claims or private land
Collect loose material only
Pack out all trash
nearest_services: Hawthorne, Nevada approximately 25 miles south. Gas, food, water, and lodging available. Mina, Nevada approximately 30 miles north, also has limited services.
accessibility_notes: Moderate terrain with uneven, rocky footing and moderate slopes. Not suitable for wheelchair access. Sturdy hiking boots with good ankle support strongly recommended. No designated trails; cross-country navigation required. High elevation (5,396 ft) may affect those with altitude sensitivity. Bring plenty of water as no water sources available on-site.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find rockhounding at Thorne, Nevada?
faq.answer: At Thorne you can find quartz, agate, chalcedony, and jasper. The high desert geology produces quality specimens suitable for lapidary work and collectors. Agate nodules are particularly common in wash bottoms and erosional areas. Most specimens are small to medium-sized, with occasional larger pieces after seasonal rain events.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to collect rocks at Thorne BLM land?
faq.answer: No permit is required for personal use collecting on this BLM land. However, you must follow the 25 lb per day limit under FLPMA regulations. Collection is free and open to the public for non-commercial use. Commercial collection is not allowed.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding near Thorne, Nevada?
faq.answer: April - May and September - October are ideal. The 5,396 ft elevation makes summers extremely hot and winters cold with potential snow. Spring and fall offer comfortable temperatures for spending extended time in the field. Avoid summer heat and winter snow conditions.

faq.question: Is Thorne rockhounding site family-friendly and suitable for beginners?
faq.answer: Yes, Thorne is excellent for families with children 8 and older and beginner rockhounders. The moderate terrain and free access make it welcoming. The dispersed BLM site allows casual surface collecting without technical skills. However, children should wear sturdy boots and stay hydrated in the high desert environment.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do I need for rockhounding at Thorne?
faq.answer: Bring a small rock hammer or chisel, classification screens for sifting wash material, sturdy hiking boots, a backpack, and abundant water. A GPS unit is helpful for navigation on unmarked BLM land. Sun protection (hat, sunscreen) is essential at 5,396 ft elevation. Bring containers for specimens and pack out all trash.

faq.question: How far from Hawthorne is Thorne rockhounding area and what are access roads like?
faq.answer: Thorne is approximately 25 miles north of Hawthorne via Nevada State Route 95, then east on rough BLM access roads. Roads are unimproved dirt with potential washboard conditions. A high-clearance vehicle is recommended; 4WD may be needed after wet weather. Plan extra time for slower driving speeds on BLM access roads.
